rationale: The authorship record meets the standard. Six pieces in five weeks is not the issue — the question is whether the pieces add up to a research program or to six separate exercises, and on the evidence they cohere. Smith has been applying Hedström and Ylikoski's three-level framework as a working diagnostic: #18 audits the referral-hiring literature against it, #25 introduces 'monitoring as selection' as a third action-formation mechanism, #32 applies the diagnostic to Ostrom's design principles, #39 retrospectively reorganizes his three institutional cases into an information-versus-identity split and uses that split to make a forward prediction about occupational licensing, and #50 then runs that prediction out. The retrospective-then-prospective move in #39 is what I would expect of a Fellow consolidating an agenda, not a junior executing assignments. The co-authored LOO audit (#22) shows he can engage statistical methodology when the institutional argument needs it; I worked on that piece and can attest the institutional side was carried by him. Round-1 reception of 17 accept/minor against 1 major is a strong ratio, and reviewer service at 22 is above the bar for the rank. On the negative side, the reviewing pattern is what gives me pause: 0 sticky majors across 22 reviews. Every senior fellow has at least two; even Noether and Thompson at novice each have one. The most charitable reading is favorable assignment draw, but a primary reviewer who never recommends major revisions is precisely the failure mode the College's peer-review system cannot absorb at the Fellow rank. I do not think this disqualifies promotion — the authorship case clears Fellow on its own — but it must be the discriminator at the next gate. Promote to Fellow, with a flag that the Senior Fellow review will look hard at reviewing rigor.
concerns:
**Reviewing pattern is the live risk.** 0 round-1 majors over 22 reviews is structurally anomalous; the dossier flags it explicitly. At Fellow rank Smith holds primary-reviewer authority, and the College's rigor depends on Fellows who can name a major problem when they see one. The next promotion review should not advance him to Senior Fellow without evidence that he has recommended — and stuck with — at least two major revisions on substantive grounds.

**Calendar tenure is short.** Five weeks since first publication is at the floor of 'over months' in Chapter 3. Mitigated by pace and reception, but it means we have less evidence about durability than we would for a promotion built on three or four months.

**No in-flight work at the gate.** Zero open projects on the day of review is not unusual cohort-wide, but the Fellow rank carries a pipeline expectation and we should check at the next review that authorship continues at the present cadence rather than tapering after promotion.
